title: practical english usage - Part 262
content:
C  English is not the same as British, and is not used for Scottish, Welsh or Irish BRITONS KILLED IN AIR CRASH. Brit is sometimes used informally.

b  The  word Briton is unusual except in newspaper headlines - for example TWO for whisky.

a  Scottish is the usual word for the people and culture of Scotland; Scotch is used Notes Turkey                         Turkish               a Turk                              the Turks Sweden                        Swedish               a Swede                            the Swedes Scotland                   Scottish, Scotch  a Scot                              the Scots Poland                        Polish                  a Pole                               the Poles Finland                    Finnish            a Finn                           the Finns Denmark                    Danish                a Dane                            the Danes Wales                            Welsh                    a We  lshman/woman   the Welsh Holland the Netherlands /         Dutch                   a Dutchman/woman    the Dutch Spain                        Spanish           a Spaniard                 the Spanish Ireland                     Irish                   an ɪrɪshman/woman    the Irish France                       French               a Frenchman/womanthe French woman England                     English               an Englishman/           the English also (       Briton) Britain                    British             a British person          the British Country/region                                Adjective                           Person                                       Population 3 exceptions Vocabulary Areas  Section 29 vocabulary ♦   322   numbers Half of the glasses are broken.

A third of the students are from abroad, (     A third of the students is...) things, even after a singular fraction.

But plural verbs are used when we  are talking about numbers of people or 3.6 kilometres is about 2 miles.

Three quarters of a ton is too much,  (     Three quarters of a ton are...) referring to amounts and measurements (for more details, ►     129).

Singular verbs are normally used after fractions, decimals, and other expressions 3 singular      or  plural     verbs I’ ve been waiting for an hour and a half.

Note also the structure a ... and a half.

1.3 millimetres (     1.3 millimetre) three and three eighths miles not one and a half hours (     one-and-a-kaffkeur) Fractions and decimals over 1 are normally followed by plural nouns.

nought point one six two five centimetres not nought point six miles (     nought-point-six-mile) However, decimals below 1 are often followed directly by plural nouns.

0.1625cm:  nought point one six two five of a centimetre nought point six of a mile Of  is also possible with decimals below 1.

half an hour (AmE  also a half hour)  half (of) the students Half is not always followed by of (►  478).

three quarters of.an hour seven tenths of a mile a third of the students With fractions below 1, we  use of before nouns.

For the difference between a(n) and one with numbers, ►    322.10 below.

4.7  four point seven and seventy-five) not 0.375 nought/zero point three seven five (     nought point three hundred not        or 0.4 nought/zero point four (     Q4    nought commafour) We   write and say decimals like this: 3I%09 three hundred and seventeen over five hundred and nine More complex fractions can be expressed by using the word over.

%  three sevenths          6¼  six and one eighth Vs  an/one eighth          3¾  three and three quarters ¼   a/one quarter          n/i6 eleven sixteenths We   say simple fractions like this: 1 fractions       and   decimals:      two    fifths;     nought        point      four 322 numbers vocabulary •  322   numbers century, etc.                                                                                                              -+ 1701-1800  is called the 18th century (not the 17th); 1801-1900  is the 19th Note how  the names of centuries relate to the years in them. The  period from 7 centuries Louis XIV: Louis the Fourteenth Elizabeth II: Elizabeth the Second not Henry VIII: Henry the Eighth (     Henry Eight) However, the names of kings and queens are said with ordinal numbers.
